content.text: An
evaluation

(eval) is a structured test that measures how well a language model performs on a defined task. You provide a set of inputs, the model produces outputs, and a scoring function determines how many outputs meet the quality bar. The result is a number you can track, compare, and use to make decisions.

Without evals, LLM development is essentially subjective: you make a change, test it on a few examples, judge the results by eye, and decide whether to ship. This works until it doesn't — until a change that seemed to improve one case silently broke twenty others, or until you need to explain to a stakeholder why the new prompt is better than the old one.

attrs.alt: Eval-driven development cycle: Define → Build → Run → Interpret → Improve → repeat

content.text: The diagram shows the five-step eval-driven development cycle. The critical insight is that the cycle starts at step 1 — defining the task and what counts as correct — not at step 5 (improve). Evals written before the prompt is written give you an objective measure of whether your improvements are real.
The Core Problem Evals Solve

Language models are non-deterministic and sensitive to prompt wording in ways that are hard to predict by reasoning alone. A change that looks like an obvious improvement — adding a sentence of context, rephrasing an instruction — can degrade performance on cases the developer did not think to test manually.
The Signal-to-Noise Problem

Manual testing of LLM outputs produces noisy signal. A developer who tests 5 examples and sees 4 good outputs concludes "this prompt works." But the model might fail on 20% of real-world inputs — cases that are slightly different from the developer's test set, drawn from a distribution the developer didn't fully anticipate.
Evals solve this by making the test set
explicit, large, and reusable
. A 200-example eval set covers the real input distribution better than 5 hand-chosen examples. It runs in seconds. And it runs the same way every time, so changes are genuinely comparable.
The Regression Problem

Every improvement to an LLM system is a potential regression. A prompt change that increases accuracy on customer-service queries might decrease accuracy on technical documentation queries, if both query types are in the system's scope. Without a comprehensive eval, the regression is invisible until it appears in production.

Evals make regressions visible before deployment. When a change is made to the prompt or the model, the full eval set runs and the score is compared to the previous version. A drop in score is a signal to investigate before the change reaches users.
Primary Use Cases for Evaluations
Evals serve different purposes at different stages of LLM system development. Understanding which use case you are in shapes what your eval set should look like.
1. Prompt Development

When writing a new prompt from scratch, the eval set defines what success looks like before you write a single word of the prompt. This is counterintuitive — most developers write the prompt first and test it second — but it is the more effective order:
Write 20–50 representative input examples covering the expected distribution of real-world inputs
Write or specify the expected output for each example (what would a perfect response look like?)
Write the prompt and run the eval — the score tells you how well the prompt handles the full distribution
Iterate on the prompt; each iteration's score is directly comparable to the last

Starting with the eval set forces clarity about the task. If you cannot specify what a good output looks like for a given input, the task is underspecified — which is itself valuable information before you invest in prompt engineering.
2. Regression Testing After Changes

The most common ongoing use of evals is regression testing: verifying that a change to the system (prompt, model version, preprocessing, postprocessing) does not degrade existing behavior. A regression eval typically covers the full scope of the system, not just the area being changed.
Change type
What the eval checks
Prompt wording change
Accuracy on the full input distribution — not just cases that motivated the change
Model version upgrade
Accuracy, latency, and cost metrics across the same set of inputs
New instruction added
Does the new instruction degrade performance on cases it was not intended to affect?
Context or system prompt change
Full regression across all task types the system handles
Output format change
Whether downstream consumers that parse the output still parse correctly
3. Model Selection and Comparison

When choosing between two model versions, two providers, or two configurations, evals provide an objective basis for comparison. "Model A seems better" is not a decision basis. "Model A scores 87% on our eval set versus 81% for Model B, with 15% lower latency and 20% lower cost" is.

Model comparison evals should cover the full scope of your task, not just the cases where you expect the models to differ. A model that is much better on your anticipated use cases but much worse on edge cases may be the wrong choice.
4. Pre-Deployment Safety Checks

For systems where incorrect outputs have significant consequences — medical information, financial advice, legal summaries — evals serve as a pre-deployment gate. A safety eval typically contains adversarial inputs, edge cases, and known failure modes. A system that fails a safety eval gate should not ship, regardless of how good it looks on capability evals.
5. Production Monitoring

Evals do not stop at deployment. Production monitoring evals sample real user inputs (with appropriate privacy handling) and score model outputs on an ongoing basis. Degradation in production score signals model drift, data distribution shift, or a change in the underlying service the model interacts with.

A production accuracy score that is lower than your pre-deployment eval score is a discrepancy worth investigating. Either your eval set does not represent real-world inputs, or something changed after deployment.
Eval Metrics: What to Measure

The score an eval produces is only meaningful if the metric it uses matches the question you are trying to answer. Different tasks call for different metrics. Choosing the wrong metric produces a score that is easy to optimize but that does not reflect real-world quality.
Accuracy (Pass/Fail)

The simplest metric: each test case either passes or fails; the score is the fraction that pass. Accuracy is appropriate when correctness is binary — the model either extracted the right entity, produced the right classification, or gave the right answer.

Accuracy is computed as: number of passing cases ÷ total cases. A score of 0.87 means 87% of inputs produced a correct output. Simple, interpretable, and the right default when you have clear correctness criteria.
Precision and Recall

For classification tasks — safety classification, content moderation, entity detection — accuracy alone can be misleading when class imbalance is present. A model that always outputs "safe" achieves 99% accuracy on a dataset that is 99% safe, while completely failing at its job.
Metric
What it measures
High value means
Precision
Of the cases the model flagged as positive, what fraction were truly positive?
Low false-positive rate — model rarely cries wolf
Recall
Of all truly positive cases, what fraction did the model catch?
Low false-negative rate — model rarely misses a real case
F1
Harmonic mean of precision and recall (2·P·R / (P+R))
Model balances both — useful single-number comparison

For content safety, high recall is usually more important than high precision: a false negative (missed unsafe content) is more harmful than a false positive (flagged safe content). The metric you optimize for should reflect this asymmetry.
LLM-as-Judge Scoring

Many tasks lack a clear correct answer — a model that summarizes a document, answers a nuanced question, or generates a creative response is not obviously right or wrong. For these tasks, a second model call can act as the scorer:
LLM-as-judge scoring scales to tasks where human annotation would be prohibitively slow or expensive. The key requirement is
calibration
: validate that the judge's scores agree with human judgment on a sample of cases (typically 5–10%) before trusting it as the primary scorer.
Task-Specific Metrics
Some tasks have established domain-specific metrics. Use them when they are well-validated for your task type — but be aware of their limitations:
Metric
Task type
Limitation
BLEU / ROUGE
Translation, summarization
Measures n-gram overlap with reference; high score doesn't guarantee good quality
Exact match
QA with short answers, slot filling
Fails on semantically equivalent but lexically different correct answers
CodeBLEU
Code generation
Syntactic similarity to reference; doesn't check if the code runs or is correct
Human preference rate
Open-ended generation
Ground truth but slow and expensive; use for calibrating automated metrics
Dev Set vs. Test Set: The Eval Split

The single most important organizational practice for evals is maintaining a clean split between the test cases used during development and a held-out set used only for final measurement. Without this split, your eval score measures "how well this prompt was tuned to these cases" rather than "how well this prompt generalizes to new inputs."
The Overfitting Problem

Every prompt iteration is implicitly influenced by the eval cases the developer has seen. If you test on the same 50 cases you used to tune the prompt, you will overfit to those 50 cases. The score will look good on those cases and may be significantly lower on the 51st case — which is a real production input.

This is the same problem as model overfitting in supervised learning, applied to prompt engineering. The solution is the same: keep a held-out test set that you only run when you want a final, unbiased measurement.
Practical Splits
Set
Size
When to use
Rules
Dev set
60–70% of eval cases
Every iteration during prompt development
Run freely; add new cases here when you find failures
Validation set
20–30% of eval cases
Before major changes (model switch, major prompt refactor)
Run at milestones, not every iteration
Test set (held out)
10–20% of eval cases
Pre-deployment final measurement only
Never use to inform prompt changes; score is the ground truth

When a team's pre-deployment test score is always within 1–2% of their dev score, that is a sign the test set is leaking into development — the cases are known and the prompt is tuned to them. A clean test set should produce a score 3–7% below the dev score on average.
Evals in the CI/CD Pipeline

For teams building LLM applications at scale, evals should run automatically — just like unit tests run in CI for traditional software. Integrating evals into the deployment pipeline prevents regressions from reaching production and gives every pull request a measurable signal.
Eval-in-CI Architecture
Developer commits a prompt or model configuration change to a feature branch
CI runs the dev eval set against the new configuration (using a smaller, fast model for development-speed feedback)
CI reports the score alongside the pull request — reviewers see 'Eval score: 84% (was 87% on main)'
A score drop above a configured threshold (e.g., > 2%) blocks the pull request until the regression is investigated
After the pull request is approved and merged, the validation set runs — a further quality gate before deploying to production
On production deployment, the test set runs one final time to establish the official pre-deployment accuracy baseline

# .github/workflows/eval.yml (example CI integration)
name: Eval Gate
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Install deps
        run: pip install anthropic pytest

      - name: Run dev eval
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python evals/run_eval.py \
            --config prompts/current_config.json \
            --dataset evals/dev_set.jsonl \
            --threshold 0.82 \
            --output eval_results.json

      - name: Report results
        run: python evals/report_results.py eval_results.json
        # Fails the CI job if score < threshold

The threshold is a team decision: too high and every iteration fails; too low and regressions slip through. A starting point is 2–3 percentage points below the current main-branch score — enough slack to avoid blocking on noise, tight enough to catch real regressions.
Eval Speed vs. Eval Coverage
A full eval run that takes 20 minutes does not fit in a fast CI loop. Teams balance this with tiered evals:
Fast eval (2–5 min):
50–100 cases, run on every commit; uses a faster/cheaper model for the scorer; catches obvious regressions
Full eval (15–30 min):
500–1000 cases, run on pull request merge; catches subtler regressions across the full distribution
Comprehensive eval (hours):
10,000+ cases, run before major releases; measures accuracy across the long tail of the input distribution
What Evals Are Not
Evals are a powerful tool, but they have specific failure modes that are important to understand before building a large eval infrastructure.
Evals Are Not Infallible

An eval is only as good as its test set and its scoring function. A 95% score on an eval set that does not represent real-world inputs is not a 95% accuracy on real-world inputs — it is a 95% accuracy on the test set. The quality of the eval depends on:
Input coverage:
does the test set reflect the real distribution of inputs the system will receive, including the long tail of unusual cases?
Scoring accuracy:
does the scoring function correctly identify good outputs from bad ones? An automated scorer that misclassifies 10% of outputs introduces 10% noise into every eval result
Label correctness:
if the expected outputs ("gold labels") are wrong, the eval penalizes correct model behavior and rewards incorrect behavior
Evals Do Not Replace Human Judgment

Evals measure what the eval measures. For tasks where quality is genuinely multidimensional — user satisfaction, coherence across a long conversation, appropriate tone for a specific audience — a single automated score will not capture all dimensions. Human evaluation remains essential for:
Calibrating automated scoring functions (validating that the automated scorer agrees with humans on a representative sample)
Evaluating dimensions that are difficult to automate (helpfulness, appropriateness, brand voice)
Investigating failures that the automated score flags but does not explain
Deciding whether an eval score change is practically significant or just noise
Eval Scores Can Be Gamed

When an eval score becomes the primary optimization target, there is pressure to optimize for the score rather than for the underlying quality the score is intended to measure. A prompt that memorizes the test set's expected outputs, or a grader that is fooled by superficially correct-looking responses, produces high eval scores without genuine quality improvement.

The defense is to maintain a separation between the eval set used during development (the "dev set") and a held-out eval set that is never used for prompt optimization (the "test set"). Score on the test set measures real capability; score on the dev set measures how well the prompt is tuned to the dev distribution.
The Eval-Driven Development Mindset
Eval-driven development is a practice, not a one-time setup. It changes how you approach every change to an LLM system:
Before any change:
run the eval, record the baseline score. A change without a baseline is not comparable to anything.
After any change:
run the eval again. Compare to the baseline. If the score improved, is the improvement large enough to be reliable (not just noise)?
When a production failure occurs:
add the failing case to the eval set before fixing it. This turns the failure into a permanent guard against regression.
When adding a new feature:
write the eval cases for the new feature before implementing the feature. If you cannot write the eval cases, the feature is underspecified.
When the eval score stops improving:
the limiting factor is probably the eval set, not the model. Audit the test cases for coverage gaps before investing more in prompt engineering.

The value of an eval set compounds over time. Each production failure caught and added to the set makes the set more representative. A system with a 500-case eval set that was built over six months of production use reflects the real input distribution far better than a 500-case set built in a single brainstorming session.
A Minimal First Eval

The perfect is the enemy of the good when it comes to evals. A minimal eval built in an afternoon is more valuable than no eval, even if it covers only 20 cases and uses a simple automated scorer. The structure of a minimal eval:

# Minimal eval structure (pseudocode)
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Test cases: list of (input, expected_output_or_criteria)
test_cases = [
    {
        "input": "Summarize this in one sentence: [long text]",
        "expected": "Contains key claim X and does not exceed 25 words"
    },
    # ... more cases
]

def score_output(model_output: str, expected: str) -> bool:
    # Simple: check for keyword presence
    # Better: use a second model call to evaluate
    return "key claim X" in model_output.lower() and len(model_output.split()) <= 25

results = []
for case in test_cases:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=100,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}]
    )
    output = response.content[0].text
    passed = score_output(output, case["expected"])
    results.append({"input": case["input"], "output": output, "passed": passed})

accuracy = sum(r["passed"] for r in results) / len(results)
print(f"Score: {accuracy:.1%} ({sum(r['passed'] for r in results)}/{len(results)} passed)")

This structure — inputs, expected outputs or criteria, a scoring function, and a reported accuracy — is the skeleton of every eval system, from a 20-case script to a 10,000-case automated pipeline. The sophistication of the grader and the size of the test set grow over time; the fundamental structure does not change.
Summary

Evaluations are the mechanism by which LLM development becomes a disciplined engineering practice rather than a craft based on intuition. They turn qualitative impressions into quantitative measurements, make regressions visible before deployment, and provide an objective basis for comparing models and prompts.
Core purpose:
measure model performance on a defined task in a way that is reproducible, objective, and comparable across changes
Primary use cases:
prompt development, regression testing, model comparison, pre-deployment safety gating, production monitoring
Key limitation:
an eval is only as good as its test set — coverage gaps and scoring inaccuracies propagate into misleading scores
Mindset shift:
write eval cases before writing the prompt; treat each production failure as an eval addition; compare every change to a baseline
Starting point:
a 20-case eval with a simple scorer is more valuable than no eval; the set grows over time as failures are captured
The next topics in this section cover how to build effective test sets, choose and implement scoring methods, and interpret eval results to guide development decisions.
